name: Cosmic balance
shortDescription: Cosmic balance, or Ma'at, is the fundamental principle of truth, justice, morality, and order in ancient Egyptian cosmology. It represents the harmonious state of the universe and the interconnectedness of all things.
description: In ancient Egyptian mythology, cosmic balance, often referred to as Ma'at, was the central principle governing the universe and human society. Ma'at embodied truth, justice, morality, and cosmic order. It was believed that maintaining Ma'at was crucial for the continued existence of the world and the well-being of its inhabitants. When Ma'at was upheld, the world flourished; when it was disrupted, chaos (Isfet) threatened to engulf everything. The pharaoh, as the divine ruler, had the primary responsibility to uphold Ma'at, ensuring justice and order throughout the land. This involved maintaining the temples, performing rituals, and enacting laws that reflected Ma'at's principles. The concept extended beyond earthly affairs, influencing the afterlife as well. In the Hall of Ma'at, the hearts of the deceased were weighed against the feather of Ma'at to determine their worthiness to enter the afterlife. A heart heavy with sin and injustice would tip the scales, leading to annihilation. Upholding Ma'at required a constant struggle against Isfet, the force of chaos and disorder. The gods, particularly Ra, Isis, and Osiris, played key roles in maintaining this balance. Ra's daily journey across the sky represented the triumph of order over darkness, while Isis's acts of compassion and protection helped to restore harmony. Osiris, as the god of the afterlife, ensured justice and order in the realm of the dead, reinforcing the importance of Ma'at even beyond earthly existence. The ankh, a symbol of eternal life, is also related as Ma'at and upholding Ma'at allowed you to achieve eternal life. The Ba, or soul, was crucial for the afterlife and its success depended on the balance of the heart during the weighing of the heart ceremony.
